---
# title: "2026 Festival of Teaching + Learning Demo Site"
# subtitle: "Bridging Theory and Practice with AI: Interactive Learning in Highly Theoretical Courses With Modern Coding Tools"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    theme: cosmo
---

::: {.hero-section}

# Bridging Theory and Practice with AI

## Interactive Learning in Highly Theoretical Courses With Modern Coding Tools

**[2026 Festival of Teaching + Learning](https://www.ualberta.ca/en/centre-for-teaching-and-learning/events/festival-of-teaching/2026/schedule.html) Demo Site**

**Dr. Tian Tian**  

Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering

University of Alberta

:::

---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}

## [Session information](https://www.ualberta.ca/en/centre-for-teaching-and-learning/events/festival-of-teaching/2026/schedule.html#MODAL-28)

**Session type:** Spark Talk — 10 minutes  

**Date:** Tuesday, April 28, 2026  

**Time:** 1:30 p.m. – 2:30 p.m.  

**Location:** Prairie Room, Lister Conference Center

:::

---

## Welcome

This demo site accompanies my Spark Talk for the **2026 Festival of
Teaching + Learning**, titled "**Bridging Theory and Practice with AI:
Interactive Learning in Highly Theoretical Courses With Modern Coding
Tools**". It introduces an interactive course-design framework that
combines **Quarto**, **marimo**, and **generative AI support** to
create browser-first learning materials for theory-intensive
engineering courses. The demo site also mimicks the actual course
outlines used in my teaching this semester.

You can find my presentation [here](./presentation/index.qmd)

---

## Presentation abstract

This Spark Talk shares my recent experiences designing course
materials for two highly theoretical engineering courses — [Mass
Transfer (CH E
318)](https://tiangroup-uofa.github.io/che318-mass-transfer/) and
[Kinetics of Materials (MAT E
664)](https://tiangroup-uofa.github.io/mate664-kinetics-of-materials/)
— using browser-first interactive course materials and generative AI
support.

I explore how tools such as marimo, developed with AI acting as a
coding and design agent, can transform traditionally abstract,
equation-heavy content into interactive, accessible learning
experiences that better connect theory with practice. These modules
emphasize interactivity in conventional engineering plots,
calculations, and simulations, while remaining fully browser-based and
requiring no additional setup. This allows students to engage with the
same materials consistently in and out of class.

I will briefly discuss how these interactive elements were
incorporated into assignments to support exploratory learning, as well
as early efforts to create an AI learning companion using Gemini
Gems to support student understanding beyond the classroom.

The session reflects a personal teaching journey toward creating
equitable, low-barrier, and versatile learning materials that help
students stay conceptually aligned across lectures, independent study,
and assessment, while offering transferable ideas for teaching
theory-intensive courses across disciplines.

---
